text: The patient is positioned under general anesthesia with a uterine manipulator in place. Three 8mm robotic trocars, including the optical trocar, are inserted at the umbilical and parumbilical areas, while additional 10mm and 5mm trocars are placed laterally for laparoscopic assistance. The robotic system is docked, and the sigmoid colon is retracted to the left side to expose the pelvis. A 5cm peritoneal incision is made at the Douglas pouch.

The surgeon performs a five-centimeter peritoneal incision at the Douglas pouch and dissects the rectovaginal space up to the perineal body. The redundant peritoneal pouch is excised, exposing a longer tract of the anterior rectum for mesh placement. A polypropylene mesh, marked with two radiopaque clips on its distal edge, is introduced into the abdominal cavity and sutured over the ventral rectum using six TRIO PDS sutures.

The surgeon dissects the rectovaginal space up to the perineal body, confirmed by digital exploration through the vagina and anorectum. The redundant peritoneal pouch is excised, allowing for a longer tract of anterior rectum for mesh placement. A polypropylene mesh, marked with two radiopaque clips on its distal edge, is introduced into the abdominal cavity and sutured over the ventral rectum using six TRIO PDS sutures.

The surgeon preserves the most proximal sutures for fixation at the proximal vagina. A small peritoneal incision, approximately three centimeters, is made at the sacral promontory. The periosteum is carefully freed, avoiding damage to the autonomic nerve fibers and lateral and utero sacral ligaments. A retroperitoneal tunnel is meticulously created from the pre-promontory incision to the pelvic incision.

The surgeon preserves the most proximal sutures for fixation at the proximal vagina. A small peritoneal incision, approximately three centimeters, is made at the sacral promontory. The periosteum is carefully freed, avoiding damage to the autonomic nerve fibers and lateral and utero sacral ligaments. A retroperitoneal tunnel is meticulously created from the pre-promontory incision to the pelvic incision.

The surgeon pulls the proximal mesh up to the sacral promontory, applying gentle tension to prevent future rectal interception and rectocele persistence. The mesh is fixed at the sacral promontory with two stitches using 2-0 PDS sutures. Two clips are placed on the mesh and another on the periosteum for potential radiographic checks of mesh detachment.

The surgeon pulls the proximal mesh up to the sacral promontory, applying gentle tension to prevent future rectal intussusception and rectocele persistence. The mesh is fixed at the sacral promontory with two 2-0 PDS sutures. Two clips are placed on the mesh and another on the periosteum for potential future radiographic evaluation.

The surgeon trims the redundant mesh and places two radiopaque clips on the mesh sides at its proximal fixation over the rectum for future radiographic checks. The posterior proximal vagina is approximated to the mesh using the two most proximal sutures, avoiding full thickness needle passage through the posterior vaginal wall. Peritoneal incisions are closed with woolock sutures, and no drain is left in place.

The surgeon trims the redundant mesh and places two radiopaque clips on the sides of the mesh at its proximal fixation over the rectum, ensuring visibility for future radiographic checks of the mesh position during follow-up.

The surgeon approximates the posterior proximal vagina to the mesh using the two most proximal sutures, ensuring careful avoidance of full-thickness needle passage through the posterior vaginal wall. The peritoneal incisions are closed with woolock sutures, and no drain is left in place.
